metric_value: 99.99% application uptime — approximately two orders of magnitude improvement over standard NT server
notes: Kastner states the $24,995 product 'adds almost two orders of magnitude to NT application availability.'
Kastner calls this 'reasonable given the value of the applications likely to be protected.'

metric_value: 4 servers: 2 lock-stepped Computing Elements (CE) + 2 I/O Processors (IOP); all Pentium Pro class
notes: CEs run lock-stepped; IOPs handle I/O; connected at NT HAL level.

metric_value: Each half connected by optical fiber up to 1.5 kilometers (1 mile) apart — building/campus-scale disaster recovery
notes: Campus-distance DR was an advanced feature; Kastner notes building fires/floods more common than generally known.

metric_value: NT OS and all shrink-wrapped applications unaware of Endurance 4000; no special application versions required
notes: Transparent fault tolerance — key differentiator from cluster software requiring special app versions.

metric_value: Recovery measured in minutes; requires reboot
source_page: Microsoft's Wolfpack is an Answer, But Not The Answer
notes: Kastner: 'minutes to recover will be good enough for many applications' but not mission-critical OLTP, system management, firewall.

metric_value: Expected to ship later in 1997
source_page: Microsoft's Wolfpack is an Answer, But Not The Answer
notes: Kastner notes Wolfpack is 'widely discussed' but not yet delivered; actual ship date September 1997 with NT Server 4.0 EE.

metric_name: Wolfpack actual ship date and reception
metric_value: Shipped September 1997 with Windows NT Server 4.0 Enterprise Edition; had significant reliability problems in v1; only 2-node clusters; improved substantially in Windows 2000
notes: Sources: Microsoft TechCommunity 2013 clustering history; Wikipedia MSCS. Kastner's caution about Wolfpack limitations proved accurate.

metric_value: Ever-increasing trend toward NT Server for enterprise applications including transaction processing, email, groupware, internet/intranet, network management
notes: Aberdeen research documenting NT adoption trajectory as of early 1997.

metric_value: NT Server was never designed to run mission-critical applications without failing; no general purpose OS makes that claim
notes: Fundamental architectural observation about NT's mission-critical fitness.

metric_value: As more enterprise application 'jewels' hosted on NT servers, risk of significant business disruption rises exponentially as servers 'deployed like popcorn'
notes: Kastner's 'paranoid IT executive' framing of the reliability risk.

metric_value: Wolfpack or other cluster software can run on Endurance-hardened servers
notes: Endurance 4000 and Wolfpack are complementary, not competing technologies.

metric_value: CEs synchronized by memory copy; IOPs synchronized by automatic disk copy when failed component replaced; true continuous processing
notes: No data loss; not even a 'hiccup' on failure — key differentiator from Wolfpack's reboot-based recovery.

metric_value: If Endurance 4000 passes hardware compatibility tests, it will be 'breakaway leader' in the new 'fault-tolerant NT servers and clusters' category
notes: Kastner's bullish commercial assessment of Marathon's market position.

metric_value: Marathon Technologies succeeded; HP announced reseller agreement for Endurance 6200 (successor) in 2000; First Options of Chicago used Marathon for trading apps; Aberdeen analyst Joe Clabby confirmed '5 nines of availability in NT industry'
notes: Sources: Computerworld June 2000 article. Marathon achieved significant commercial success as Kastner predicted.

metric_value: Pivoted to software-only product (everRun) in 2004; acquired by Stratus Technologies September 24, 2012; technology continued as Stratus everRun MX product line
notes: Sources: Computerworld Sept 2012; Stratus press release. Marathon survived ~22 years as independent company before acquisition.

metric_value: OLTP, system management, firewall applications in critical category; also many e-mail, file serving, groupware applications
source_page: Microsoft's Wolfpack is an Answer, But Not The Answer
notes: Kastner's categorization of applications requiring true fault tolerance vs. cluster-level HA.

metric_value: Compaq, Dell, HP, IBM, Micron — major Pentium Pro server platforms tested
notes: Broad hardware compatibility was important for enterprise acceptance.

metric_value: No true fault-tolerant solution existed in NT cluster market at time of study; only Wolfpack (not yet shipped) or cluster middleware requiring special app versions
source_page: Microsoft's Wolfpack is an Answer, But Not The Answer
notes: Market gap that Marathon's Endurance 4000 was positioned to fill.

metric_value: Endurance 4000 includes port to alert external alarm system upon failure
notes: Operations management integration feature.

metric_value: 'Well-architected, affordable, hardware-based solution' that adds value 'as if by magic' to shrink-wrapped applications on standard Intel hardware
notes: Strongly positive analyst endorsement — PSK's characteristic direct style.
